mn35:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Vesālī, in the Great Wood, in the Hall with the Peaked Roof.
mn35:10.1: “An analogy occurs to me, Master Gotama.”
mn35:10.2: “Let it occur to you, Aggivessana,” said the *Bhagavā*.
mn35:10.3: “Just as, Master Gotama, whatever seed-grown and plant-grown things there are that attain growth, increase, and fullness, all of them do so depending on the earth and grounded on the earth.
mn35:10.4: Thus these seed-grown and plant-grown things attain growth, increase, and fullness.
mn35:10.5: Or just as, Master Gotama, whatever tasks of strength are done, all of them are done depending on the earth and grounded on the earth.
mn35:10.6: Thus these tasks of strength are done.
mn35:10.7: Just so, Master Gotama, as *rūpa*, this individual person, standing established on *rūpa*, produces merit or demerit; as *vedanā*, this individual person, standing established on *vedanā*, produces merit or demerit; as perception, this individual person, standing established on perception, produces merit or demerit; as *saṅkhāras*, this individual person, standing established on *saṅkhāras*, produces merit or demerit; as consciousness [*viññāṇa*], this individual person, standing established on consciousness [*viññāṇa*], produces merit or demerit.”
mn35:11.2: ‘*Rūpa* is my self, *vedanā* is my self, perception is my self, *saṅkhāras* are my self, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is my self’?”
mn35:11.3: “Indeed, Master Gotama, I do say this:
mn35:11.4: ‘*Rūpa* is my self, *vedanā* is my self, perception is my self, *saṅkhāras* are my self, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is my self’; and so does this large crowd.”
mn35:11.5: “But what will this large crowd do for you, Aggivessana?
mn35:11.6: Come now, Aggivessana, untangle just your own assertion.”
mn35:11.7: “Indeed, Master Gotama, I do say this:
mn35:11.8: ‘*Rūpa* is my self, *vedanā* is my self, perception is my self, *saṅkhāras* are my self, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is my self.’”
mn35:12.1: “Then, Aggivessana, I will question you about just that, and you should answer it as seems right to you.
mn35:12.10: to have someone executed, or fined, or banished.
mn35:12.11: How much more then an anointed noble king, such as King Pasenadi of Kosala or King Ajātasattu Vedehiputta of Magadha?
mn35:12.12: He would, Master Gotama, and he deserves to exercise it.”
mn35:12.3: would an anointed noble king have sovereignty in his own realm—
mn35:12.4: to have someone executed, or fined, or banished, as King Pasenadi of Kosala, or King Ajātasattu Vedehiputta of Magadha?”
mn35:12.5: “He would, Master Gotama, an anointed noble king would have sovereignty in his own realm—
mn35:12.6: to have someone executed, or fined, or banished, as King Pasenadi of Kosala, or King Ajātasattu Vedehiputta of Magadha.
mn35:12.8: that is, the Vajjis and the Mallas—
mn35:12.9: exercise sovereignty in their own realm—
mn35:13.10: A second time too Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, fell silent.
mn35:13.11: Then the *Bhagavā* said this to Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son:
mn35:13.12: “Now answer, Aggivessana; now is not the time for your silence.
mn35:13.13: Anyone, Aggivessana, who, when asked a legitimate question by the Tathāgata up to the third time, does not answer—right here his head splits into seven pieces.”
mn35:13.3: ‘*Rūpa* is my self,’ do you exercise mastery over that *rūpa*—
mn35:13.4: ‘Let my *rūpa* be thus; let my *rūpa* not be thus’?”
mn35:13.5: When this was said, Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, fell silent.
mn35:13.6: A second time the *Bhagavā* said this to Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son:
mn35:13.7: “What do you think, Aggivessana, when you say this:
mn35:13.8: ‘*Rūpa* is my self,’ do you exercise mastery over that *rūpa*—
mn35:13.9: ‘Let my *rūpa* be thus; let my *rūpa* not be thus’?”
mn35:14.1: Now at that time the spirit Vajirapāṇi, holding an iron thunderbolt—blazing, burning, and all aflame—was standing in the air above Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son:
mn35:14.2: “If this Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, when asked a legitimate question by the *Bhagavā* up to the third time, does not answer, right here I will split his head into seven pieces.”
mn35:14.3: And both the *Bhagavā* and Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, saw that spirit Vajirapāṇi.
mn35:14.4: Then Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, frightened, alarmed, with hair standing on end, sought only the *Bhagavā* for protection, sought only the *Bhagavā* for shelter, sought only the *Bhagavā* for refuge, and said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn35:14.5: “Let Master Gotama question me; I will answer.”
mn35:15.3: ‘*Rūpa* is my self,’ do you exercise mastery over that *rūpa*—
mn35:15.4: ‘Let my *rūpa* be thus; let my *rūpa* not be thus’?”
mn35:16.3: What you say later does not fit with what you said earlier, nor what you said earlier with what you say later.
mn35:16.6: ‘*Vedanā* is my self,’ do you exercise mastery over that *vedanā*—
mn35:16.7: ‘Let my *vedanā* be thus; let my *vedanā* not be thus’?”
mn35:17.3: What you say later does not fit with what you said earlier, nor what you said earlier with what you say later.
mn35:17.6: ‘Perception is my self,’ do you exercise mastery over that perception—
mn35:17.7: ‘Let my perception be thus; let my perception not be thus’?”
mn35:18.3: What you say later does not fit with what you said earlier, nor what you said earlier with what you say later.
mn35:18.6: ‘*Saṅkhāras* are my self,’ do you exercise mastery over those *saṅkhāras*—
mn35:18.7: ‘Let my *saṅkhāras* be thus; let my *saṅkhāras* not be thus’?”
mn35:19.3: What you say later does not fit with what you said earlier, nor what you said earlier with what you say later.
mn35:19.6: ‘Consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is my self,’ do you exercise mastery over that consciousness [*viññāṇa*]—
mn35:19.7: ‘Let my consciousness [*viññāṇa*] be thus; let my consciousness [*viññāṇa*] not be thus’?”
mn35:2.1: Now at that time Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, was residing at Vesālī, a debater, regarded as learned, esteemed by many people.
mn35:2.2: He would speak thus in the assembly at Vesālī:
mn35:2.3: “I do not see any ascetic or brahmin, any leader of an order, leader of a group, or teacher of a group, even one claiming to be an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened one, who, when engaged by me in debate, would not tremble, shake, and quiver, and from whose armpits sweat would not be released.
mn35:2.4: If I were to engage even a senseless pillar in debate, then when engaged by me in debate even that would tremble, shake, and quiver.
mn35:2.5: What then of a human being?”
mn35:20.10: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
mn35:20.16: what do you think, Aggivessana, is consciousness [*viññāṇa*] permanent or impermanent [*aniccaṁ*]?”
mn35:20.20: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *dukkha*, and subject to change, is it fitting to regard that thus:
mn35:20.21: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
mn35:20.3: What you say later does not fit with what you said earlier, nor what you said earlier with what you say later.
mn35:20.9: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *dukkha*, and subject to change, is it fitting to regard that thus:
mn35:21.2: someone attached to *dukkha*, gone to *dukkha*, immersed in *dukkha*, regarding *dukkha* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’—could he himself fully understand *dukkha*, or could he dwell having brought *dukkha* to an end?”
mn35:21.6: are you not, in that case, attached to *dukkha*, gone to *dukkha*, immersed in *dukkha*, regarding *dukkha* thus:
mn35:21.7: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
mn35:21.8: “How could it not be so, Master Gotama?
mn35:21.9: That is indeed so, Master Gotama.”
mn35:22.1: “Just as, Aggivessana, a man seeking heartwood, searching for heartwood, wandering in search of heartwood, might take a sharp axe and enter a forest.
mn35:22.10: But now, Aggivessana, some drops of sweat have been released from your forehead, having soaked through your upper robe and fallen to the ground.
mn35:22.11: But now, Aggivessana, there is no sweat on my body.”
mn35:22.12: So the *Bhagavā* uncovered his golden-colored body in that assembly.
mn35:22.13: When this was said, Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, sat silent, downcast, shoulders drooping, face lowered, brooding, unable to respond.
mn35:22.2: There he might see a great banana trunk, straight, young, and newly grown.
mn35:22.3: He would cut it at the root, and having cut it at the root, he would cut off the top; having cut off the top, he would strip off the leaf-sheaths.
mn35:22.4: While stripping off the leaf-sheaths there, he would not even find pith, much less heartwood.
mn35:22.5: In just the same way, Aggivessana, when you are pressed by me on your own doctrine, pursued, and examined, you are empty, hollow, and mistaken.
mn35:22.6: Yet this statement was spoken by you, Aggivessana, in the assembly at Vesālī:
mn35:22.7: ‘I do not see any ascetic or brahmin, any leader of an order, leader of a group, or teacher of a group, even one claiming to be an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened one, who, when engaged by me in debate, would not tremble, shake, and quiver, and from whose armpits sweat would not be released.
mn35:22.8: If I were to engage even a senseless pillar in debate, then when engaged by me in debate even that would tremble, shake, and quiver.
mn35:22.9: What then of a human being?’
mn35:23.1: Then Dummukha, the Licchavi’s son, knowing that Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, was silent, downcast, shoulders drooping, face lowered, brooding, unable to respond, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn35:23.10: And now, *bhante*, Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, is unable to approach the *Bhagavā* again with a view to debate.”
mn35:23.3: “Let it occur to you, Dummukha,” said the *Bhagavā*.
mn35:23.4: “Just as, *bhante*, not far from a village or town there might be a lotus pond.
mn35:23.5: And in it there might be a crab.
mn35:23.6: Then, *bhante*, many boys or girls might come out from that village or town and approach that lotus pond. Having approached it, they would go down into the pond, lift that crab out of the water, and place it on dry land.
mn35:23.7: Whatever limb that crab might extend, those boys or girls would cut it off, break it, and smash it with a stick or a potsherd.
mn35:23.8: Thus, *bhante*, that crab, with all its limbs cut off, broken, and smashed, would be unable to go down again into that pond as before.
mn35:23.9: In just the same way, *bhante*, whatever crookednesses, twistings, and writhings belong to Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, all of them have been cut off, broken, and smashed by the *Bhagavā*.
mn35:24.1: When this was said, Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, said this to Dummukha, the Licchavi’s son:
mn35:24.10: He sees with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] as it truly is any consciousness [*viññāṇa*] whatsoever—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near—as: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn35:24.11: To this extent, Aggivessana, my disciple is one who acts in accordance with the teaching, who follows the advice, who has crossed over doubt, gone beyond perplexity, attained confidence, and dwells independent of others in the Teacher’s instruction.”
mn35:24.2: “Wait, Dummukha, wait, Dummukha, … we are not discussing with you; here we are discussing with Master Gotama.
mn35:24.3: Let that be the talk of us and of other ordinary ascetics and brahmins.
mn35:24.4: I take it to be mere lamentation.
mn35:24.5: But to what extent is Master Gotama’s disciple one who acts in accordance with the teaching, who follows the advice, who has crossed over doubt, gone beyond perplexity, attained confidence, and dwells independent of others in the Teacher’s instruction?”
mn35:24.6: “Here, Aggivessana, my disciple sees with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] as it truly is any *rūpa* whatsoever—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near—as: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn35:25.1: “But to what extent, Master Gotama, is a *bhikkhu* an *arahant*, with *āsavas* destroyed [*khīṇāsavo*], who has lived the holy life, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, reached his own goal, with the fetters of existence entirely destroyed, completely liberated through final knowledge?”
mn35:25.2: “Here, Aggivessana, a *bhikkhu*, having seen with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] as it truly is any *rūpa* whatsoever—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near—as: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self,’ is liberated through non-clinging [*anupādā*].
mn35:25.6: Having seen with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] as it truly is any consciousness [*viññāṇa*] whatsoever—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near—as: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self,’ he is liberated through non-clinging [*anupādā*].
mn35:25.7: To this extent, Aggivessana, a *bhikkhu* is an *arahant*, with *āsavas* destroyed [*khīṇāsavo*], who has lived the holy life, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, reached his own goal, with the fetters of existence entirely destroyed, completely liberated through final knowledge.
mn35:26.1: With mind liberated [*vimuttacitto*] in this way, Aggivessana, a *bhikkhu* is endowed with three unsurpassables—
mn35:26.2: the unsurpassable of seeing, the unsurpassable of practice, and the unsurpassable of liberation.
mn35:26.3: With mind liberated [*vimuttacitto*] in this way, Aggivessana, a *bhikkhu* honors, respects, esteems, and venerates the Tathāgata alone—
mn35:26.4: ‘The *Bhagavā* is awakened; he teaches *dhamma* for awakening. The *Bhagavā* is tamed; he teaches *dhamma* for taming. The *Bhagavā* is peaceful; he teaches *dhamma* for settling [*samathāya*]. The *Bhagavā* has crossed over; he teaches *dhamma* for crossing over. The *Bhagavā* is fully extinguished; he teaches *dhamma* for final *nibbāna* [*parinibbānāya*].’”
mn35:27.1: When this was said, Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn35:27.2: “It is we ourselves, Master Gotama, who are ruined; we were rash, we who thought that we could attack Master Gotama in debate.
mn35:27.3: For a man might perhaps come to safety after attacking a rutting elephant, but after attacking Master Gotama a man would not come to safety.
mn35:27.4: For a man might perhaps come to safety after attacking a blazing mass of fire, but after attacking Master Gotama a man would not come to safety.
mn35:27.5: For a man might perhaps come to safety after attacking a venomous snake of terrible poison, but after attacking Master Gotama a man would not come to safety.
mn35:27.6: It is we ourselves, Master Gotama, who are ruined; we were rash, we who thought that we could attack Master Gotama in debate.
mn35:27.7: Let Master Gotama accept tomorrow’s meal from me together with the *bhikkhu saṅgha*.”
mn35:28.1: Then Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, knowing that the *Bhagavā* had consented, addressed those Licchavis:
mn35:28.2: “Let the good Licchavis hear me: the ascetic Gotama has been invited by me for tomorrow, together with the *bhikkhu saṅgha*.
mn35:28.3: Therefore you should bring me whatever you think appropriate for that.”
mn35:29.1: Then, when that night had passed, those Licchavis brought to Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, five hundred dishes of cooked food for the meal offering.
mn35:29.2: Then the Nigaṇṭha’s son, having had fine foods, both to chew and to eat, prepared in his own park, informed the *Bhagavā* of the time:
mn35:29.3: “It is time, Master Gotama; the meal is ready.”
mn35:3.1: Then in the morning Venerable Assaji dressed, took his bowl and robe, and entered Vesālī for alms.
mn35:3.2: Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, saw Venerable Assaji coming in the distance while he was walking up and down and wandering about in Vesālī for exercise.
mn35:3.3: Seeing him, he approached Venerable Assaji, and on approaching, he exchanged greetings with Venerable Assaji.
mn35:3.4: When they had concluded courteous and memorable conversation, he stood to one side. Standing to one side, Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, said this to Venerable Assaji:
mn35:30.1: Then in the morning the *Bhagavā* dressed, took his bowl and robe, and went to the park of Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son. On arriving, he sat down on the seat prepared, together with the *bhikkhu saṅgha*.
mn35:30.2: Then Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, with his own hands served and satisfied the *bhikkhu saṅgha* headed by the Buddha with fine foods, both to chew and to eat.
mn35:30.3: Then Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, when the *Bhagavā* had eaten and withdrawn his hand from the bowl, took a certain low seat and sat down to one side.
mn35:30.4: Sitting to one side, Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn35:30.5: “As for whatever merit and great merit there is in this giving, may that be for the happiness of the donors.”
mn35:30.6: “Aggivessana, whatever comes from relying on such a recipient of offerings, not free from lust, not free from hatred, not free from delusion—that will be for the donors.
mn35:30.7: But whatever comes from relying on a recipient of offerings like me, free from lust, free from hatred, free from delusion—that will be for you.”
mn35:30.8: The Shorter Discourse to Saccaka is finished, the fifth.
mn35:4.1: “But how, Master Assaji, does the ascetic Gotama train his disciples, and what kind of instruction often circulates among the ascetic Gotama’s disciples?”
mn35:4.2: “Thus, Aggivessana, the *Bhagavā* trains his disciples, and this kind of instruction often circulates among the *Bhagavā*’s disciples:
mn35:4.3: ‘*Rūpa*, *bhikkhus*, is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *vedanā* is impermanent [*aniccā*], perception is impermanent [*aniccā*], *saṅkhāras* are impermanent [*aniccā*], consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*].
mn35:4.4: *Rūpa*, *bhikkhus*, is not-self [*anattā*], *vedanā* is not-self [*anattā*], perception is not-self [*anattā*], *saṅkhāras* are not-self [*anattā*], consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is not-self [*anattā*].
mn35:4.5: All *saṅkhāras* are impermanent [*aniccā*], all *dhammas* are not-self [*anattā*].’
mn35:4.6: Thus, Aggivessana, the *Bhagavā* trains his disciples, and this kind of instruction often circulates among the *Bhagavā*’s disciples.”
mn35:4.7: “It is badly heard indeed, Master Assaji, that we have heard the ascetic Gotama to hold such a doctrine.
mn35:4.8: Perhaps someday or other we might meet Master Gotama; perhaps there might be some discussion; perhaps we might draw him away from that bad view.”
mn35:5.1: Now at that time about five hundred Licchavis had assembled in the council hall for some business.
mn35:5.10: Just as a sixty-year-old bull elephant, having entered a deep lotus pond, would play the game called hemp-washing,
mn35:5.11: so too I think I will play the ascetic Gotama as a game called hemp-washing.
mn35:5.12: Come forth, good Licchavis, come forth, good Licchavis. Today I will have a discussion with the ascetic Gotama.”
mn35:5.2: Then Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, approached those Licchavis, and on approaching, he said this to them:
mn35:5.3: “Come forth, good Licchavis, come forth, good Licchavis. Today I will have a discussion with the ascetic Gotama.
mn35:5.4: If the ascetic Gotama stands by his position as it was set forth to me by one of his well-known disciples, the *bhikkhu* named Assaji, then just as a strong man might grab a long-fleeced ram by the fleece and drag it about, haul it about, and tug it all around,
mn35:5.5: so too I will drag the ascetic Gotama about in debate, haul him about, and tug him all around in debate.
mn35:5.6: Just as a strong brewer’s worker might throw a large brewer’s mat into a deep pond, seize it by the handles, and drag it about, haul it about, and tug it all around,
mn35:5.7: so too I will drag the ascetic Gotama about in debate, haul him about, and tug him all around in debate.
mn35:5.8: Just as a strong brewer’s rogue might seize a strainer by the edge and shake it, rattle it, and thrash it,
mn35:5.9: so too I will shake the ascetic Gotama in debate, rattle him, and thrash him.
mn35:6.2: “Will the ascetic Gotama get the better of Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, in debate, or will Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, get the better of the ascetic Gotama in debate?”
mn35:6.4: “Who is this Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, that he should get the better of the *Bhagavā* in debate? Rather, the *Bhagavā* will get the better of Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, in debate.”
mn35:6.5: Then Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, surrounded by about five hundred Licchavis, approached the Hall with the Peaked Roof in the Great Wood.
mn35:7.1: Now at that time many *bhikkhus* were walking up and down in the open air.
mn35:7.2: Then Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, approached those *bhikkhus*, and on approaching, he said this to them:
mn35:7.3: “Where, sirs, is that Master Gotama now dwelling?
mn35:7.4: For we wish to see Master Gotama.”
mn35:7.5: “There, Aggivessana, the *Bhagavā* has entered the Great Wood and is sitting at the root of a certain tree for the day’s dwelling.”
mn35:8.1: Then Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, together with a large Licchavi assembly, entered the Great Wood and approached the *Bhagavā*. On approaching, he exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*.
mn35:8.2: When they had concluded courteous and memorable conversation, he sat down to one side.
mn35:8.3: And those Licchavis too, some having paid homage to the *Bhagavā*, sat down to one side; some exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*, and after concluding courteous and memorable conversation, sat down to one side. Some, raising their joined palms toward the *Bhagavā*, sat down to one side; some, having announced their name and clan in the *Bhagavā*’s presence, sat down to one side; some sat down to one side in silence.
mn35:9.1: Sitting to one side, Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn35:9.2: “I would ask Master Gotama something, if Master Gotama would give me the opportunity for the answering of the question.”
mn35:9.4: “But how does Master Gotama train his disciples, and what kind of instruction often circulates among Master Gotama’s disciples?”
mn35:9.5: “Thus, Aggivessana, I train my disciples, and this kind of instruction often circulates among my disciples:
mn35:9.6: ‘*Rūpa*, *bhikkhus*, is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *vedanā* is impermanent [*aniccā*], perception is impermanent [*aniccā*], *saṅkhāras* are impermanent [*aniccā*], consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*].
mn35:9.7: *Rūpa*, *bhikkhus*, is not-self [*anattā*], *vedanā* is not-self [*anattā*], perception is not-self [*anattā*], *saṅkhāras* are not-self [*anattā*], consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is not-self [*anattā*].
mn35:9.8: All *saṅkhāras* are impermanent [*aniccā*], all *dhammas* are not-self [*anattā*].’
mn35:9.9: Thus, Aggivessana, I train my disciples, and this kind of instruction often circulates among my disciples.”
